---
title: "Data"
---

Every dataset here is public and free, with no login and no paid access. Where a
source would normally require accepting terms or running a manual download, this
build avoids it by using a small committed table instead, and the notes below say
exactly what to replace if you want to extend the project.

## Base geography

Country shapes and coastlines come from **Natural Earth**, loaded through the
`rnaturalearth` package. Natural Earth is in the public domain, and because the
package ships the data, there is nothing to download by hand.

## Energy infrastructure

The infrastructure layer is a small committed table of the region's LNG import
terminals, `data/processed/lng_terminals.csv`. It holds the name, country,
location, annual regasification capacity, status, and a source note for each
terminal.

The capacities are taken from operator reporting and the **Global Energy
Monitor** terminal tracker, current as of 2024 to 2025. The `source` column in
the table names the operator behind each figure. Two points worth flagging:

- Capacities move. Świnoujście reached 8.3 bcm only after its third tank came
  online in early 2025, and Mukran's 13.5 bcm assumes both of its FSRUs running.
- The Paldiski jetty in Estonia is left out on purpose. It is shelved, and its
  capacity figure refers to the same FSRU (Exemplar) that now operates at Inkoo,
  so including both would count one vessel twice.

To extend the project, **EMODnet Human Activities** and **OpenStreetMap** (via
the `osmdata` package) are the route to add pipelines and cables as line
features, and the **Global Energy Monitor** tracker is the source to reconcile
the capacities against in full.

## Proximity reference

Instead of a maritime boundary line, the proximity measure uses distance to the
nearest **Russian territory**, meaning the mainland near the Gulf of Finland plus
the Kaliningrad exclave, taken from Natural Earth. It is reproducible from a
public package and easy to read: how far each terminal is from Russian soil.

## Dependence

The dependence layer uses gas import dependency from **Eurostat** (indicator
`nrg_ind_id`, natural gas, 2023), held in `data/processed/import_dependence.csv`.
The figure is the share of gas demand met by net imports. Values above 100%
reflect stock draws or re-exports in that year, and Denmark is low because of its
own North Sea production. `scripts/03_eurostat.R` reproduces the pull.

## A note on conflict data

This project frames the question as exposure and structural dependence, not
active conflict, because there is no battlefield on the Baltic seabed. ACLED is
deliberately not used, since it now requires registration, which would break the
public data rule. If a later version needs conflict events, UCDP GED is the
public, no-login substitute.

## How the data is prepared

The steps that turn the tables and geometry into analysis-ready layers live in
`scripts/01_clean.R`, and the Analysis page repeats them at render time:

- standardise the columns,
- project everything to ETRS89 / LAEA Europe (EPSG:3035) so distances come out in
  kilometres,
- clip the geography to the Baltic study area,
- keep the capacity and country information on the terminal layer.

## Citations

- Natural Earth. Free vector and raster map data, 1:50m cultural vectors.
  <https://www.naturalearthdata.com> (accessed June 2026).
- Eurostat. Energy imports dependency by products, indicator `nrg_ind_id`,
  natural gas, 2023. <https://ec.europa.eu/eurostat> (accessed June 2026).
- Global Energy Monitor. Global LNG Terminal Tracker.
  <https://globalenergymonitor.org> (accessed June 2026).
- Operator figures for capacity and status: Gaz-System, Deutsche ReGas, Gasgrid
  Finland, KN Energies, and Hamina LNG (2024 to 2025 reporting).

If you add pipelines or cables later: EMODnet Human Activities
(<https://emodnet.ec.europa.eu/en/human-activities>) and OpenStreetMap.
